title: Capstone Project & Pro Roadmap
subtitle: Graduation day! Build a real program and step out as a Python programmer.
goal: Recap everything you learned, build a full Quiz Game capstone using functions, lists, dicts, loops, and a class, and get a roadmap for what to learn next.

blocks.heading: You made it to Day 22! 🎉
blocks.html:
Twenty-two days ago you printed your first Hello, world!. Today you're going to build a complete program from scratch. That's a huge leap — be proud of it. 🏆

This is graduation day. We'll look back at the whole journey, build a real Quiz Game, and then point you toward the exciting road ahead.

blocks.heading: Look how much you know now 🧠
blocks.html:
You started with nothing and now you can do all of this:

  • print() and comments — talking to the screen
  • Variables & types — strings, numbers, booleans
  • Strings — slicing, f-strings, methods like .upper()
  • Lists, tuples & dictionaries — storing lots of data
  • if / elif / else — making decisions
  • for & while loops — repeating work
  • Functions — packaging code you can reuse
  • Files — reading and writing data
  • Classes & objects — designing your own types
  • Errors — try/except to handle problems gracefully

That is a real programmer's toolkit. Time to use all of it at once.

blocks.heading: The Capstone: a Quiz Game 🎯
blocks.html:
We'll build a quiz game that asks questions, keeps score, and grades you at the end. It uses dictionaries for questions, a list to hold them, a class to run the game, loops to go through questions, and conditionals to check answers.

We'll build it in three pieces. First, the data.

blocks.caption: Piece 1 — the questions (a list of dictionaries)
blocks.explain: Each question is a dictionary with the question text, a list of choices, and the index of the correct answer. We put all of them in one big list so we can loop over them.

blocks.caption: Piece 2 — a class that runs the quiz
blocks.explain: The class bundles the data (questions, score) with the actions (ask, play). __init__ sets things up, ask() shows one question and checks it, and play() loops through every question and prints the final score.

blocks.html: Why a class? A class keeps related data and behavior together. self.score belongs to the game and follows it around — no messy global variables.

blocks.caption: Piece 3 — put it all together
blocks.explain: This is the whole capstone in one runnable block: data + class + a play() call. Lists, dicts, a class, a loop, an if-check, and f-strings — every Day-22 skill working together.

blocks.question: In our quiz game, why did we store each question as a dictionary?
options: Because dictionaries run faster than the computer
So we can label each part: the question, the choices, and the answer
Dictionaries are the only thing Python can loop over
blocks.explain: A dictionary lets us give each piece a clear name (key) like "q", "choices", and "answer" — much clearer than remembering positions.

blocks.question: What does the play() method's for loop do?
options: Goes through each question one at a time
Prints the score before any questions
blocks.explain: for item in self.questions: visits each question in the list, one after another, so every question gets asked.

blocks.title: Add your own question
blocks.instructions: Add a third dictionary to the questions list with a "q", a "choices" list, and an "answer" index. Keep using the dict shape we used.
blocks.starter:
questions = [
    {"q": "def is used for?", "choices": ["loops", "functions"], "answer": 1},
    # add your own question dictionary below
]
print("Total questions:", len(questions))
blocks.solution:
questions = [
    {"q": "def is used for?", "choices": ["loops", "functions"], "answer": 1},
    {"q": "What color is the sky?", "choices": ["green", "blue"], "answer": 1},
]
print("Total questions:", len(questions))
blocks.passMsg: Your quiz is growing — that's how real apps get built!
blocks.failMsg: Make sure your new question is a dictionary with "q", "choices", and "answer" keys.

blocks.title: Give the game a method
blocks.instructions: Add a method called total_questions to the class that returns how many questions there are. Use len() on self.questions.
blocks.passMsg: You added behavior to your own class — pro move!
blocks.failMsg: Define def total_questions(self): inside the class and return len(self.questions).

blocks.heading: Where to go next 🚀
blocks.html:
You've outgrown the browser sandbox — time to run Python for real! Here's your roadmap:

  • Install Python from python.org so it lives on your own computer.
  • Pick an editor: Thonny is perfect for beginners; VS Code is what the pros use.
  • Build projects you actually care about — that's how learning sticks.
  • Learn a library for your interest: websites → Flask or Django; data & charts → pandas; games → pygame; automating boring tasks → plain scripts.
  • Keep practicing on sites like Codewars, Exercism, or Advent of Code.
  • Read other people's code on GitHub — you learn tons by seeing how others solve problems.

🎓 You did it. Twenty-two days ago Python was a mystery. Now you can store data, make decisions, repeat work, write functions, design classes, and build a whole program. You are a Python programmer now — go build something amazing. 🐍🏆

challenge.title: Finish the Quiz Game 🏆
challenge.instructions: Complete the QuizGame so it really works! Inside play(), loop through self.questions, add 1 to self.score for each correct answer (treat the first choice, index 0, as correct for this challenge), then print the final score as 'Score: X'. Finish with a print that celebrates finishing the course.
challenge.hint: Use a for loop over self.questions, an if to check the answer, self.score += 1 to count points, and an f-string to print 'Score:' with the total.
challenge.passMsg: 🏆🎓 INCREDIBLE! You built a complete program AND finished all 22 days. You are officially a Python programmer. Now go build the world!
